allies.rationale_text: System-2 is the essential cognitive mode for applying base rates — slow, reflective, contemplative processing is required to invoke reference-class thinking and override the vivid specifics that System 1 uses to neglect base rates.
allies.source_quote: Applying base rates is a process that requires effort, logic, and deliberation, classifying it as a System 2 function (slow, reflective, contemplative thinking). Consciously invoking System 2 is necessary to override the quick, intuitive responses of System 1 that often lead to base-rate neglect.

allies.rationale_text: The scientific method supports base rates by forcing beliefs to be calibrated against repeatable historical evidence instead of vivid specifics.
allies.source_quote: Base rates embody a scientific mindset by demanding that beliefs be calibrated based on objective, repeatable evidence (historical frequency).

antagonists.rationale_text: System-1's fast, intuitive processing is the primary cognitive source of base-rate neglect — heuristic shortcuts substitute vivid, representative details for statistical regularities, causing people to ignore base rates when System 1's automatic narrative feels compelling.
antagonists.source_quote: System 1 Thinking (Heuristics and Biases): System 1 is fast and automatic, relying on heuristics and biases that can lead to base-rate neglect. These shortcuts substitute vivid, representative details for statistical regularities.

antagonists.rationale_text: Einstellung-effect (cognitive entrenchment) causes experts to dismiss base-rate data that contradicts their specialized mental models — the fixation on domain-specific frameworks blocks the reference-class correction that base rates provide.
antagonists.source_quote: Cognitive Entrenchment: Experts who have deep mastery of a field may suffer from cognitive entrenchment, making them less flexible and more prone to ignore external, general data (base rates) that contradict their internal specialized mental models.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Base-rates conflicts with representativeness-heuristic when vivid case details crowd out statistical regularities.
structured_tensions.source_quote: base-rates conflicts with representativeness-heuristic (representativeness heuristic) when the representativeness heuristic often leads to base-rate neglect by focusing on specific details rather than statistical regularities.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Base-rates conflicts with confirmation-bias when prior beliefs override inconvenient outside-view frequencies.
structured_tensions.source_quote: base-rates conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when confirmation bias can cause individuals to ignore base rates in favor of information that confirms their pre-existing beliefs.

curation_notes.summary: The source is strong on explicit tensions, but most ally and antagonist prose does not map cleanly to the current canonical id surface without forcing outside-view or System 1 approximations.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor-added relation overlays beyond the raw ally, antagonist, and structured tension sections.
open_questions: Cognitive entrenchment wired in Task 2.5 sweep via einstellung-effect mapping.
System-2 wired as ally, system-1 wired as antagonist (Task 4.1 sweep). Outside-view remains deferred — embedded in base-rates itself, no clean standalone canonical id.

allies: The raw ally section is richer than the resolved target-id surface and explicitly names the outside view, System 2 thinking, and analogy/reference classes.
antagonists: The raw source explicitly names System 1 thinking, motivated reasoning, and cognitive entrenchment, but this pilot did not force those into approximate target ids.
